attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Continue OKKI CRM data scraping project — diagnose and fix Chrome CDP debug port not listening, then locate the API endpoint returning all 432 contacts</request>
  <investigated>
Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) port-mode failure: Chrome launched from %LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe with --remote-debugging-port=9222 flag present on all child processes, but port 9222 never listens and no DevToolsActivePort file is created. Remaining hypotheses: ① generic port-mode failure (Chrome build or policy issue) ② profile/lock corruption (cleaned singleton locks, no effect) ③ corporate-managed Chrome with debugging disabled.

Pipe-mode CDP as fallback: Created test_pipe_clean.js to spawn Chrome with --remote-debugging-pipe + clean temp user-data-dir, testing whether CDP works over stdin/stdout when TCP port binding fails. Result pending.

Browser-side interception as CDP-independent alternative: Pivoted to inject_intercept.js — a DevTools Console paste script that hooks window.fetch and XMLHttpRequest in-page to capture all xiaoman.cn API traffic. This bypasses CDP entirely and requires only a logged-in browser session.
  </investigated>
  <learned>
Chrome --remote-debugging-port=9222 can silently fail without visible error — child processes inherit the flag but CDP never initializes. The absence of DevToolsActivePort file is the definitive diagnostic signal.

Pipe mode (--remote-debugging-pipe) communicates CDP over process stdio rather than TCP, avoiding port-binding issues entirely.

The inject_intercept.js pattern (in-page fetch/XHR hooking) is a zero-risk alternative to CDP-based interception: requires no port, no protocol, no authentication, and leaves no trace after close.

Long-term target architecture: once the 432-contact API endpoint is identified, the final scraping solution will extract cookies from Chrome's profile and make direct HTTP requests — no browser, no CDP, no Playwright needed.

OKKI CRM is hosted at crm.xiaoman.cn and uses the "云超数码" account.
  </learned>
  <completed>
- Cleaned Chrome singleton lock files (SingletonLock, SingletonSocket, SingletonCookie, lockfile) — no effect on CDP issue
- Created D:\ClaudeWorkspace\08_客户跟进\Okki\scripts\test_pipe_clean.js — tests whether --remote-debugging-pipe works with clean temp profile
- Created D:\ClaudeWorkspace\08_客户跟进\Okki\scripts\inject_intercept.js — browser console injection script (production-ready): hooks fetch/XHR, floating UI panel, one-click JSON export, zero outbound requests, fully restorable
- diagnose_api.js is pre-written and ready to execute once CDP or pipe-mode connection is established
- State documented: CDP port mode broken, pipe mode being tested, inject script ready as CDP-independent path
  </completed>
  <next_steps>
User is being instructed to: ① reopen Chrome logged into OKKI CRM ② paste inject_intercept.js into DevTools Console ③ browse customer management pages while monitoring the floating panel for the 432-record API ④ click "导出JSON" to download the captured API dump. Once the target API endpoint is identified, work shifts to building a Node.js-based direct HTTP scraping solution using extracted Chrome profile cookies — no browser/CDP dependency.
  </next_steps>
  <notes>
This is a two-phase strategy: Phase 1 (in-progress) uses in-page interception to discover the API endpoint as a one-shot reconnaissance mission. Phase 2 (planned) replaces browser automation with direct HTTP requests using session cookies extracted from the Chrome profile — making the solution cron-friendly and zero-dependency on browser state. The inject_intercept.js script is designed as a single-use exploration tool, not the final scraping solution. The CDP debugging path is effectively paused in favor of the faster console-injection approach.
  </notes>
</summary>
